comments.author: @rickj8859
comments.text:
Mush, I don't know where you got your distorted Prague (and Czech) history from.  That Godless comment was rather strange, seeing that Prague churches, by comparisson with London churches, are packed.  Large number of London churches were converted to coffee shops, libraries and lately into Mosques.
By comparison, Prague and Bohemia are still largely Catholic, although that people don't necessarily broadcast it.
As well, as far as education is concerned?  I had seen the OECD education tables for 2003 where Czechs had 3rd highest level of education in the world after Koreans and Japanese.  In 2023 they allegedly dropped behind Singapore and Norway.  Czechs have very low number of 3rd world migrants, bar the 1+ million of Ukrainian refugees.  Hence they have 99% literacy, unlike the vast majority of West European countries where literacy drops down to 70% .... and they publish fake figures to hide it.
Prague is an old medieval city and it remained the Capital of Kingdom of Bohemia since 8th century AD.  In the Middle Ages, Prague was the Capital of Holy Roman Empire ... right upto Prague defenestration.  Prague was at cross roads from East to West and North to South which gave it a mixture of architecture and cultures.  The Pilsner lager that you have mentioned, or Budweiser are just 2 out of hundreds of breweries, and the date relates to one brewery.  Beer has been brewed around Bohemia lot longer than 200 years.  You have mentioned Svitschkova and Gulash as being the most popular faire in Czech homes, or restaurants.  I beg to differ.  Czechs make 20+ different type of dumplings, some from potato flour stuffed with ham, some from milk and flour stuffed with fruit .... and they do have Svitschkova once a month, perhaps.  Whenever I visit Prague, I stay at Imperial Art Deco Hotel and when I eat outside hotel, I go to Zvonice and have a veal Schitzel, or go to a nearby pub, where they do a mean Bramborak with 12 oz cooked ham for 250 Kč.   Czechs eat lot of poultry, fish, game, quite a bit of pork etc.  Svitschkova is filet of beef, served cold, thinly sliced, and covered in thick hot creamy sauce with  bread and flour dumplings.   Germans love  that stuff more then Czechs do .... and I know that Americans in general hate it.   Goulash is just a thick Hungarian soup.  Czechs do eat it, just as they eat beef steak or home made sausage, or hamburger.   When it comes to food, that you haven't mentioned and Czechs are famous for it is sausages.   Czechs invented what Americans call Frankfurters.
An other thing that you have mentioned is Franz Kafka, who was just an insignificant little pencil pusher and definitely not a  writer.    Kafka was obviously unhappy in his job and not too keen on Austrian burocracy so he wrote few little stories for the local Jiddish newspaper.  He spoke Jiddish German and I doubt that he spoke a word in Czech.   Prague was a bilingual city, as was Vienna and Kafka lived in the Jewish quarter of the town  
The next topic mentioned was Ice Hockey.  Czechs (Bohemia) won the European championship first time in 1910 and number of years after that.  After 1945 they won the world ice hockey championships as Czechoslovakia and later on Czech Republic, roughly 15x.  In fact, Czechoslovakia & Czechia combined are the 3rd most successful country after Canada and USSR & Russia.   You have briefly mentioned football as not being very popular.   Well it is.   Czechs have won European Championships and were 2x in other finales and they were 2x in world championships finale in 1934 and 1962 as well.  Czechs tend to do well in volleyball and in tennis.   So for not such a little country (there are many smaller countries in EU) and only 11 million people, they are doing relatively well.
One thing that you failed to spot ...  and that relates to Prague more than everywhere in Bohemia.  Prague population hates tourists just as passionately as Parisians and Venetians do.   Tourists are pests, responsible for high prices  in shops and restaurants and for ridiculously high rental prices.  If you don't mind traveling half an hour out of Prague, you will pay 80% less for similar accommodation in one of the smaller satellite towns.  By comparisson with London?  2 bed apartment on perimeter of Greater London is $3,000 and the same apartment in Downtown London is $10,000 to $15,000 per month.  Czech wages vary,  they are on average close to Austrian wages and about 30% less than German wages.  But you are right not many people make more than $20,000 per month.

comments.author: @WorldwideLifeVentures33
comments.text: An outstanding script that dares to step outside Prague's noisy tourist traps. Unmasking the trdelník myth and decoding the initial guardedness of the Czechs shows deep research. The portrayal of fiercely independent, educated Czech women, alongside their quiet genius (inventing soft contacts and the word "Robot"), is brilliant. A truly high-caliber cultural deep-dive!

comments.author: @yasenikdevizin9883
comments.text:
Карловы Вары сами чехи называют -- Ивановы Вары.
Почему?

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text:
Yes, some Czechs jokingly call Karlovy Vary “Ivanovy Vary” because for many years the town had a very strong Russian-speaking presence, especially among tourists, spa visitors, investors, shops, and hotel owners. So the nickname is a humorous way of saying that the city sometimes felt more Russian than Czech in certain areas.

Karlovy Vary is still a beautiful Czech spa town with a deep history, but this Russian influence became a very noticeable part of its modern image.

comments.author: @jiritichy6855
comments.text: The beer is no more cheap.  3, 4 or even 5 Dollars for a beer is not heap! It used to be cheap! During the communism, the beer was about 20x cheaper!    In a IV class of restaurant (workers category) the 1/2 litre beer jug used to cost 1.70Kcs, which translates to about 7 US Cents! That is right-seven Cents!!!....Now that was cheap! The cost of water is 0. They pump it from underground for free.(cannot be tap water). And the cost of ingredients went up only somewhat.....nothing major!  The only cost, which went up is the labor and equipment. But equipment lasts for 30,40, 50 years! and the production is nearly automated......so there is not much of the workers involved anymore.  It is just that todays companies are doing it for the maximum profit....

comments.author: @MageSkeleton
comments.text:
The Czech Republic operates as a unitary parliamentary republic where executive power is exercised by the Government led by the Prime Minister, who is currently Andrej Babiš as of December 2025.  The President, currently Petr Pavel, serves as the ceremonial head of state and Commander-in-Chief, elected by direct popular vote for a five-year term. 

This sounds promising. 

A unitary parliamentary republic is a form of government where a country operates as a unitary state (central government holds supreme authority) and a parliamentary republic (executive branch derives legitimacy from and is accountable to the legislature).  In this system, voters elect members of parliament, who then select the head of government and cabinet, while the head of state is often elected by parliament or holds a ceremonial role.

Something seems off about this system, it wants to be a "republic" but i fail to see the "republic" part of this system. Who's doing the voting? if the government holds "supreme authority" what's stopping it from fixing elections? Hopefully someone can enlighten everyone. This video doesn't address any of these concerns.

comments.author: @erikl1447
comments.text: I visited Prague in 1995.  I was stunned by it's beauty.  It was also strange to see how it expressed both it's medieval roots and it's Soviet republic past.  The olympic stadium is beyond massive.  I also remember you could only get 2 types of beer....light or dark.  And the beer was fantastic.  Got a simple meal of sauerkraut soup with pretzel and it was so delicious.  The WW2 museum was brutally honest....some of the pictures they showed would never be shown in a western museum.  And they had a bar that targeted US crowds....we went in and they were showing The Simpsons on a TV.  The perfect taste of home when we really needed it during our trip.  They did have a lot of gypsies but only the kids would bother you for money or handouts.

I'm writing from an undisclosed location about 20km from the Czech border.
I love the Czech people (the girls a bit more than the guys) and the country.
And the beer.
And the festival scene.
And Praha.
And the rich history and past - Jan Hus for example was the prime reformer, hundred years before Martin Luther even, while Frank Zappa went on to become 'cultural ambassador' under Havel! Who else can claim things like that ; )
Much love to all of you!
Thanks for the great vid!
May you live long and bring forth several hockey teams of kids ; )
